graph_vector_fulltext.message:
A distributor, importer, deployer, or other third party can be treated as a “provider” of a high-risk AI system where “certain specific conditions” are met (the text in the provided excerpt flags that such conditions exist and are meant to ensure legal certainty, but it does not enumerate them). The context also notes that an operator can hold multiple roles at once (e.g., distributor and importer) and must then fulfil all obligations cumulatively for those roles; if they are deemed a provider under those specific conditions, they would also take on provider obligations for the high-risk AI system.

This links to “substantial modification” because substantial modification is a concept used with regard to high-risk AI systems and “may require” a (new) conformity assessment procedure. In addition, substantial modifications “must be registered” in the EU database, so if a distributor/importer/deployer makes a substantial modification that results in the system being high-risk (or changes its high-risk status), that is the type of situation that can trigger treating that actor as the provider, with the associated conformity-assessment and registration consequences.

If you can share the exact article/paragraph you’re looking at (or more surrounding text), I can list the precise “specific conditions” for when the role-switch to “provider” happens, because they are not included in the provided context snippet.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: Under what circumstances does a distributor, importer, or deployer become considered a 'provider' of a high-risk AI system, and how does this relate to the substantial modification concept?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
A distributor, importer, deployer, or other third party can be treated as a “provider” of a high-risk AI system where “certain specific conditions” are met (the text in the provided excerpt flags that such conditions exist and are meant to ensure legal certainty, but it does not enumerate them). The context also notes that an operator can hold multiple roles at once (e.g., distributor and importer) and must then fulfil all obligations cumulatively for those roles; if they are deemed a provider under those specific conditions, they would also take on provider obligations for the high-risk AI system.

This links to “substantial modification” because substantial modification is a concept used with regard to high-risk AI systems and “may require” a (new) conformity assessment procedure. In addition, substantial modifications “must be registered” in the EU database, so if a distributor/importer/deployer makes a substantial modification that results in the system being high-risk (or changes its high-risk status), that is the type of situation that can trigger treating that actor as the provider, with the associated conformity-assessment and registration consequences.

If you can share the exact article/paragraph you’re looking at (or more surrounding text), I can list the precise “specific conditions” for when the role-switch to “provider” happens, because they are not included in the provided context snippet.

Content: Text Content:
 considered to be the provider for the purposes of this Regulation,  and when that provider has not expressly excluded the change of the AI system into a high-risk AI system, the  former provider should nonetheless closely cooperate and make available the necessary information and provide the  reasonably expected technical access and other assistance that are required for the fulfilment of the obligations set  out in this Regulation, in particular regarding the compliance with the conformity assessment of high-risk AI  systems. (87) In addition,
----
 certain situations those  operators could act in more than one role at the same time and should therefore fulfil cumulatively all relevant  obligations associated with those roles. For example, an operator could act as a distributor and an importer at the  same time. (84) To ensure legal certainty, it is necessary to clarify that, under certain specific conditions, any distributor, importer,  deployer or other third-party should be considered to be a provider of a high-risk AI system
----
 where necessary, the power to adopt acts in  accordance with Article 290 TFEU should be delegated to the Commission to amend the conditions under which an  AI system is not to be considered to be high-risk, the list of high-risk AI systems, the provisions regarding technical  documentation, the content of the EU declaration of conformity the provisions regarding the conformity assessment  procedures, the provisions establishing the high-risk AI systems to which the conformity assessment procedure  based on assessment of the quality management
----
 public, providers of high-risk AI systems other than those related to products falling within  the scope of relevant existing Union harmonisation legislation, as well as providers who consider that an AI system  listed in the high-risk use cases in an annex to this Regulation is not high-risk on the basis of a derogation, should be  required to register themselves and information about their AI system in an EU database, to be established and  managed by the Commission. Before using an AI system listed
----

chunks.text: certain situations those operators could act in more than one role at the same time and should therefore fulfil cumulatively all relevant obligations associated with those roles. For example, an operator could act as a distributor and an importer at the same time. (84) To ensure legal certainty, it is necessary to clarify that, under certain specific conditions, any distributor, importer, deployer or other third-party should be considered to be a provider of a high-risk AI system

considered to be the provider for the purposes of this Regulation, and when that provider has not expressly excluded the change of the AI system into a high-risk AI system, the former provider should nonetheless closely cooperate and make available the necessary information and provide the reasonably expected technical access and other assistance that are required for the fulfilment of the obligations set out in this Regulation, in particular regarding the compliance with the conformity assessment of high-risk AI systems. (87) In addition,

where necessary, the power to adopt acts in accordance with Article 290 TFEU should be delegated to the Commission to amend the conditions under which an AI system is not to be considered to be high-risk, the list of high-risk AI systems, the provisions regarding technical documentation, the content of the EU declaration of conformity the provisions regarding the conformity assessment procedures, the provisions establishing the high-risk AI systems to which the conformity assessment procedure based on assessment of the quality management

public, providers of high-risk AI systems other than those related to products falling within the scope of relevant existing Union harmonisation legislation, as well as providers who consider that an AI system listed in the high-risk use cases in an annex to this Regulation is not high-risk on the basis of a derogation, should be required to register themselves and information about their AI system in an EU database, to be established and managed by the Commission.
